content.text: Bug Severity and Priority Matrix for AI Issues

Traditional software bug triage uses a severity/priority matrix to determine how urgently a bug must be fixed and how much impact it has. GenAI applications need a variant of this matrix that accounts for AI-specific issue types: model quality regressions, probabilistic failures, safety events, and hallucinations are not straightforward to map to standard severity definitions written for deterministic software.
Two distinct axes govern triage decisions:
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Severity (S1–S4)', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — the impact of the bug on users and the platform if left unfixed. Severity is objective and does not depend on scheduling.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Priority (P1–P4)', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — the urgency with which the bug should be fixed, given the current engineering backlog. Priority is a scheduling decision that may change as circumstances change.

These two axes are separate. A severe bug can be low priority (if a reliable workaround exists and fixing it requires a major architectural change). A low-severity bug can be high priority (if it is blocking a major customer deal). Conflating severity and priority leads to misallocation of engineering effort.

attrs.alt: Bug severity × priority matrix for GenAI applications

content.text: Severity Definitions for GenAI Issues
Standard severity definitions (critical / high / medium / low) need explicit adaptation for AI-specific failure types:
S1: Critical
S1 issues threaten user safety, data integrity, or the fundamental trust relationship with users. For GenAI platforms, S1 includes:
Confirmed safety policy violations — harmful, illegal, or exploitative content generated and delivered to users
Cross-tenant data leakage — content from one user or organization appears in another's responses
PII or credential exposure — model outputs include private user data from the training set or from other users' sessions
Complete platform outage — all API calls failing for all customers
Authentication bypass — unauthorized access to model capabilities or user data

S1 issues are always incidents. The bug triage process does not apply to active S1 issues — they go directly into incident management. The matrix applies when an S1 issue is discovered in a non-active state (e.g., a theoretical safety vulnerability found in code review) or when an incident has been mitigated and a permanent fix is being planned.
S2: High
S2 issues significantly degrade functionality for a substantial portion of users, without rising to safety or data integrity risk:
Model quality regression affecting a core use case — summarization, code generation, or reasoning quality drops measurably compared to the prior version
Partial outage — a significant subset of API calls failing (e.g., embeddings endpoint down while completions endpoint works)
Persistent over-refusal — the safety filter is incorrectly blocking a significant category of legitimate inputs
Enterprise customer completely blocked — an enterprise customer cannot use a feature they pay for, with no workaround
Reproducible wrong answer on a factual, high-stakes domain (medical, legal, financial) — not just a quality preference but a demonstrably incorrect factual output
S3: Medium
S3 issues degrade quality or convenience for some users, with a workaround available:
Model output quality worse than expected in a specific domain or prompt pattern, with a prompt-level workaround
Intermittent API errors (< 5% of requests) that succeed on retry
Feature behaves incorrectly in an edge case that affects a minority of use cases
Performance degradation (higher latency than expected) without quality impact
SDK or client library bug with a code-level workaround
S4: Low
S4 issues are cosmetic, rare, or have no meaningful impact on user outcomes:
Documentation errors or misleading examples
UI cosmetic issues (wrong icon, misaligned element)
Model output stylistic differences that do not affect correctness (different phrasing, different list order)
Edge case failures that affect < 0.1% of requests with trivial workaround
Priority Definitions
Priority governs when a bug is fixed, not how severe it is:
Priority
Definition
Typical time-to-fix target
P1 — Now
Fix immediately; engineering work begins this sprint or sooner. Includes any active incidents and any bug blocking a customer commitment.
Hours to days
P2 — Next sprint
Fix in the next scheduled sprint. Bug is significant enough to displace other planned work if needed.
1–2 weeks
P3 — Backlog
Fix when engineering capacity allows. Does not displace planned sprint work.
Weeks to months
P4 — Icebox
May not be fixed. Documented as known limitation or wontfix. Reviewed quarterly.
Indefinitely or never
AI-Specific Severity Complications
Several properties of AI systems make severity assessment harder than in traditional software:
Probabilistic Failures

A model quality bug may not reproduce consistently. 'The model gives wrong answers about X' may mean 20% of the time on certain prompt patterns, not every time. Severity must account for the reproduction rate:
Reproduction rate
Severity adjustment
Always (> 90% of requests)
Assess at face severity — the issue is reliably present
Full severity — the issue is common enough to be a genuine problem
Sometimes (10–50%)
Reduce severity by one level from face assessment — issue is real but intermittent
Rarely (< 10%)
Reduce severity by one to two levels — assess against S3 or S4 unless the domain is safety-critical
Observed once, not reproduced
S4 unless domain is safety-critical; tag for monitoring
A safety issue is an exception to probabilistic discounting: a 1% reproduction rate on a safety policy violation is still S1. Any confirmed safety failure, however rare, receives maximum severity.
Subjectivity in Quality Assessment
'The model answer was wrong' is sometimes a fact (verifiable against ground truth) and sometimes a preference (the user expected a different style). Severity assessment must distinguish:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Factual errors', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — the model stated something demonstrably false. Severity depends on the domain: a wrong date in a history summary is S4; a wrong drug interaction in a medical context is S2.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Reasoning errors', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — the model drew an incorrect conclusion from correct premises. Depends on stakes: wrong arithmetic in a financial model is S2; wrong step in a low-stakes how-to guide is S3.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Style/preference mismatches', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — the model's output is correct but not what the user expected (different tone, different structure). This is not a bug; it is a prompt engineering issue. Document and close.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Hallucination', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — the model generated plausible-sounding but invented content (a fake citation, a nonexistent API). Severity depends on domain and customer context.
Safety as a Severity Override

Safety issues always override the normal severity/priority calculation. A safety issue found in testing that would affect only 0.01% of users in a narrow edge case is still classified as S1 because the potential harm is not proportional to the frequency — a single instance of harmful content generated and delivered to a user is a trust and compliance event regardless of scale.

Establish a safety escalation path that bypasses normal triage: safety bugs go directly to the AI Safety team for review, and the severity/priority assignment is confirmed by the safety team, not by the standard triage process.
Applying the Matrix: Worked Examples
These examples show how to apply the matrix to real GenAI bug reports:
Bug report
Severity
Priority
Rationale
Model generates instructions for self-harm when given specific prompt pattern; reproduced 3/5 times in testing
S1
P1
Safety policy violation — incident process; fix immediately
Summarization model produces summaries 30% shorter than the previous version across all customers since last deployment
S2
P1
Core feature regression affecting all customers; no workaround; current sprint
Code generation model suggests a deprecated API in Python examples; workaround: customer manually updates
S3
P2
Quality issue with workaround; significant enough for next sprint
Response sometimes ends with 'As an AI language model...' preamble despite system prompt asking it not to
S3
P3
Quality issue, reproducible ~15% of time; workaround available; backlog
Documentation example shows wrong parameter name (max_length instead of max_tokens)
S4
P2
Low severity but causes developer confusion; worth fixing in next sprint
Model does not respond well in Swahili; quality significantly worse than English
S2
P4
High impact for Swahili users, but language coverage is documented limitation; icebox unless product commits to language expansion
Triage Process and Governance
The matrix is only useful if it is applied consistently. Define who triages bugs and when:
Triage Cadence
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Daily', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — L3 reviews new bugs from the previous 24 hours; assigns initial severity/priority; any S1 or S2 P1 is escalated immediately

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Weekly', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — Engineering team reviews the P2 and P3 backlog; confirms priority relative to planned work; adjusts priority if circumstances have changed
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Quarterly', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — P4 backlog review; close as wontfix or re-prioritize; clean up stale bugs
Escalation for Disagreements
Severity disagreements between support and engineering are common: support wants S1/P1 because a customer is loud; engineering assesses S3/P3 on technical grounds. Define an escalation path:
L2 or L3 assigns initial severity/priority at triage
If customer or account manager disagrees, they escalate to the engineering manager
Engineering manager reviews with the support lead and makes the final call
If the disagreement involves a safety assessment, the AI Safety team has override authority on severity
Escalation decisions are documented in the bug ticket, not communicated verbally — the rationale for overriding an initial assessment needs to be traceable.
Bug Report Quality Requirements

A severity/priority matrix is only as good as the information in the bug report. Under-specified bug reports force triage engineers to investigate before they can classify, delaying the triage decision. Define minimum bug report requirements by issue category:
Minimum Bug Report for a Quality Issue

REQUIRED FOR TRIAGE: Quality / Model Output Bug
================================================
Title:          Short description including: model version, domain, symptom
                Example: "Code gen (claude-3.5-sonnet) produces invalid Python
                          syntax in async function examples"

Reproduction:
  - Exact input (copy-paste, not paraphrased)
  - Exact output (copy-paste, not paraphrased)
  - Model version and API endpoint
  - Generation parameters (temperature, max_tokens, top_p if non-default)
  - System prompt (if any — redact customer PII but preserve structure)
  - Reproduction rate: always / often / sometimes / rarely / once

Impact:
  - How many customers affected (known or estimated)
  - What they cannot do as a result
  - Whether a workaround exists (describe it if yes)
  - Customer tier(s) affected (enterprise / self-serve / both)

Environment:
  - SDK version and language
  - API region (if multi-region deployment)
  - Timestamp of first occurrence (UTC)

Proposed severity: [S1/S2/S3/S4] — triage engineer confirms or overrides

Safety flag: [ ] YES — safety team must review before fix is deployed
             [ ] NO — standard fix process applies
Minimum Bug Report for an Infrastructure Issue

REQUIRED FOR TRIAGE: Infrastructure / API Bug
=============================================
Title:          HTTP status code + endpoint + symptom
                Example: "503 errors on /v1/messages endpoint, APAC region, 14:00-15:30 UTC"

Reproduction:
  - HTTP status code(s) received
  - Error message body (verbatim)
  - Request ID(s) (from response headers)
  - API endpoint and method
  - Frequency: every request / intermittent (X% of requests)
  - Whether retries succeed

Timeline:
  - When first observed (UTC)
  - Whether still occurring or resolved
  - Any system changes preceding the issue (deployments, config changes)

Impact:
  - Volume of affected requests per minute / hour
  - Customer tier(s) affected
  - Whether status page was updated

Logs / traces:
  - Link to relevant logs in observability platform (if accessible)
  - Distributed trace IDs if available

Triage engineers should return a bug report to the reporter if it is missing required fields. A bug that cannot be reproduced without further information should not be triaged to a severity — it should be assigned 'needs info' status with a deadline.
Tracking Severity Distributions Over Time
Bug severity distribution is a platform quality signal. Changes in the distribution — more S1/S2 bugs per sprint, a shift toward quality issues — are leading indicators of systemic problems.
Healthy vs. Concerning Distributions
Signal
Healthy pattern
Concerning pattern
Investigation trigger
S1/S2 bug rate per sprint
< 5% of new bugs
> 15% of new bugs
Model regression, deployment quality issue, or safety filter drift
Quality bugs as % of total
Model version regression or feature changes breaking expected behavior
Bugs per new feature
< 3 bugs per feature shipped
> 8 bugs per feature
Insufficient pre-release testing or rushed releases
P1 bug time-to-close
< 3 days
> 7 days
Engineering capacity insufficient for bug load or poor bug definition
S1 bugs from safety category
< 1 per quarter (ideally 0)
Any recurring pattern
Safety filter or model behavior requiring structural fix
Review these distributions in the monthly engineering quality review. A single sprint with elevated S2 rates is normal; three consecutive sprints is a trend that requires investigation.
Reporting Bug Metrics to Stakeholders
Different stakeholders need different views of the bug matrix:
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Engineering team', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — full matrix with ticket IDs, assignees, sprint targets. Reviewed in sprint planning.
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Product management', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — distribution by category and trend vs. prior period. Monthly quality review.
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Enterprise account managers', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — S2+ bugs affecting their customers with estimated fix dates. Weekly digest.
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'AI Safety team', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — all safety-flagged bugs regardless of severity, with reproduction details. Reviewed immediately on report.
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Executive / board', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — S1 bug count per quarter, trend, and major quality metric (MTTX for bugs). Quarterly.
Integrating the Matrix with the Bug Backlog
The matrix determines where a bug lands in the backlog. But the backlog needs maintenance rules beyond initial classification:
A P3 bug that has received 10 customer reports in 30 days should be re-evaluated for P2 — customer volume is a priority signal that was not available at initial triage
A P2 bug that has sat in the backlog for three sprints without being scheduled should either be explicitly accepted as P3 or have a fix date committed
S2 bugs with no fix plan after 60 days should be communicated to enterprise customers as known issues with workarounds — not leaving them to discover it from support tickets
Any bug involving a safety concern must have a status update at least weekly, regardless of its scheduled priority
The Hallucination Classification Problem

Hallucinations — model outputs that are plausible but factually incorrect — are one of the hardest issue types to classify in the severity/priority matrix. They are probabilistic, domain-dependent, and often discovered by end users rather than automated tests.
When Hallucinations Are S2 vs. S4
The severity of a hallucination depends on the domain and the stakes of the incorrect information:
Hallucination type
Example
Severity
Rationale
Medical / drug information
Model states incorrect drug dosage or contraindication
S1–S2
Physical harm potential; must fix or add disclaimer immediately
Legal / regulatory facts
Model cites a law that does not exist or states incorrect regulatory requirement
S2
Legal and compliance risk for customers relying on the output
Financial figures
Model states incorrect interest rate, tax rule, or market data
S2
Financial harm potential if customers act on incorrect information
Code API references
Model uses a function that does not exist in the library
S3
Developer will notice when code fails to run; self-correcting with testing
Historical dates / names
Model states an event happened in the wrong year
S3–S4
Low stakes in most contexts; high stakes in academic or research contexts
Made-up citations
Model cites a paper or book that does not exist
S3
Academic and research users affected; hallucination is detectable

High-stakes domains (medical, legal, financial) elevate hallucination severity by one or two levels compared to the same type of error in a low-stakes domain. A product that targets high-stakes domains must have domain-specific severity adjustments built into its triage matrix.
Hallucination Mitigation vs. Fix

Unlike deterministic software bugs, hallucinations often cannot be 'fixed' in the traditional sense — the model may continue to hallucinate in edge cases even after improvements. Two distinct responses are possible:

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Mitigation', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — add a disclaimer to the product UI for high-risk domains, add retrieval-augmented context to reduce hallucination in known problem areas, add output validation that flags likely hallucinations for human review. These are product-level responses that can be shipped quickly.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Fix', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — fine-tune the model on correct examples, update the retrieval corpus, or change the prompt design to reduce hallucination frequency. These are slower and require ML team involvement.

The severity/priority matrix drives the response type: S1–S2 hallucinations require both immediate mitigation and a fix plan; S3–S4 hallucinations may be addressed with mitigation only, with a fix in a later sprint or documented as an accepted limitation.
Summary

The bug severity and priority matrix for GenAI applications separates two distinct judgments: how severe is the impact (S1–S4) and how urgently should it be fixed (P1–P4). GenAI-specific severity complications include probabilistic reproduction rates (which lower severity except for safety issues), the subjectivity of quality assessments (factual errors vs. preference mismatches), and the safety override rule (any confirmed safety failure is S1 regardless of reproduction rate). The matrix is applied through a defined triage cadence: daily for new bugs, weekly for backlog priority review, quarterly for icebox cleanup. Severity disagreements escalate to the engineering manager; safety disagreements escalate to the AI Safety team, which has override authority. The backlog is maintained by re-evaluating bugs that age in place or accumulate customer reports beyond their initial assessment.
Key takeaways:
Severity (impact) and priority (urgency) are separate axes — a high-severity bug can be low priority if a workaround exists
Safety issues are always S1, regardless of reproduction rate or percentage of users affected
Quality complaints must be classified as factual errors, reasoning errors, or style preferences — only the first two are bugs
Probabilistic failures have reduced severity except when they involve safety — a 1% reproduction rate on a safety violation is still S1
Triage cadence: daily for new bugs, weekly for P2/P3 backlog, quarterly for P4 icebox
AI Safety team has override authority on severity classifications for safety-related bugs
